townAils: The bank failed and took every deed with it
Quarantine — the Army posted it, and won't say for what
The cemetery is full, and the new one keeps flooding
Half the men rode out to a strike; nothing has come back but the pay
The telegraph line is down more than it is up; the repair crews quit
A land agent is buying everything, sight unseen, cash on the barrel
The salt has gone bitter in every cellar at once
The schoolhouse stands empty; the teacher walked west mid-lesson
townSecret: The first founder is under the courthouse, and the town charter says why
Every deed in the vault burned but one, and it names ground that isn't on any map
The flooded graves are empty; they have been for years
The pay comes on schedule because someone up at the strike is signing dead men's names
The line goes down whenever it tries to carry one particular name
The agent's principal is a company; the company was dissolved in 1851
It isn't the salt. It's the water under the salt, rising
The teacher read the children something out of the old survey journal, and the children remember it
npcWant: A witness gone — paid, scared, or buried
Passage on the next coach, any seat, any price
Someone to read them a letter they can't bring themselves to open
Their claim worked one more month; the vein is close, they can hear it
A gun that doesn't miss, and they've heard where one is buried
Absolution from a particular man of God, and he's refused twice
The party's horses — offered fair, then unfair, then desperate
To know if a name means anything to you, watching your face as you answer
npcTell: Laughs a half-beat after everyone else
Keeps a coin moving over their knuckles; it stops when they lie
Never turns their back on the window
Writes everything down in a ledger nobody's allowed to touch
Flinches at church bells, covers it with a cough
Feeds every stray in town and won't own a dog
Wears mourning black for someone they won't name
Their shadow sits wrong in lamplight, and they stand where there isn't any
rumors: The stage line's paying triple for a driver on the north route; the last three quit at the same milepost
A man in town sells maps to a mine that's on no claim; every map is different, every buyer leaves happy
The river ran backward for a minute Tuesday; the ferryman won't take money anymore
There's a preacher walking the territory baptizing in dust; the baptized don't sweat after
Old Widow Pruitt's hens are laying black eggs, and she's selling them to somebody
The fort traded a whole company east midwinter; the replacements don't drill and don't drink
A card sharp at the crossing pays his debts in coin nobody minted; it spends fine, but count your fingers
They struck something soft in the new shaft, and the engineer took the first train east
The coach that went missing in '79 came in Tuesday, on schedule, driver waving. Nobody was at the reins when it stopped
There's a homestead where the corn grows in rows nobody planted, pointing at the mesa
A wolfer up the valley collected bounty on the same wolf four times; the hide walks off every winter
The county surveyor burned his own maps and won't draw the west quarter again
The bank's new vault door came from back east; the locksmith who fit it left town within the hour and hasn't been seen since
A drummer selling tonic claims it cures anything but grief; folks buy it anyway
The schoolhouse bell rings itself at midnight, three times, no more, no less
Someone's paying premium for black cats, no questions asked
The new judge never sleeps, never blinks, and never loses a case he wants to win
A homestead family sold up overnight and left the supper table set
The telegraph line hums even when the wire's down
A grave out past the church line keeps getting dug up from the inside
The saloon's mirror shows the room a half-second behind everyone in it
A well-dressed stranger's buying up debts nobody remembers owing
The livery's new stallion won't let anyone ride him after dark, and the smith won't say why he keeps him anyway
Every Sunday the church is fuller than the town has people
trailDay: A survey stake driven where no line should run, ribbon still bright
Wagon ruts that end mid-prairie — no wreck, no stock, no turnaround
A swarm of grasshoppers moving against the wind
An abandoned camp, gear costly and untouched, fire laid but never lit
A dead man's horse grazing saddled; the brand's from a county that doesn't border this one
Range cattle bunched in a perfect circle, heads out, since sunup
A peddler mending a wheel who knows the party's names, and apologizes for it
Bones in the wash, big ones, wearing a harness
A wagon train's worth of ruts, arrow-straight, that just stop
Crows following the party, keeping pace, not scavenging
A dry wash running with an inch of water nobody upstream let loose
Fence posts standing in a line with no fence, no gate, no reason
A child's shoe, alone, no tracks leading to or from it
The trail forks where the map shows none
A herd of horses, unbranded, grazing wild, spooked by nothing anyone can see
Heat shimmer that holds a shape too long to be heat
A preacher's hat, crushed flat, half-buried, no body near it
Sign of a big cook-fire, days old, enough food scraps for thirty men, one bedroll
trailNight: The coffee tastes of river water; the barrel was filled at a well
Two fires out on the flat where there should be none; near dawn they go out together
The picket line's knots are retied, better than anyone in the party ties them
A smell of a town — bread, smoke, stables — miles from any
The night birds pass overhead all night, all going the one direction
Someone's watch runs backward till dawn; it keeps fair time after
Hoofbeats circle the camp at a canter, twice, and stop where the light ends
In the morning, every track in camp is your own
The stars go out in a patch overhead, then come back wrong
A lantern bobbing along the ridge, no rider under it
Somebody's already sitting at the fire when the watch changes
The mules won't lie down all night, standing asleep on their feet
A hymn, half-remembered, carried on wind blowing the wrong direction
Frost on the saddle blankets in high summer
A second moon-shadow that doesn't match anybody's stance
The coffee pot boils dry in minutes, however much water goes in
Somebody's spurs jingle just outside the firelight, keeping time with nobody's steps
The night goes dead quiet, all at once, for exactly one held breath
plunder: A cavalry sabre, presentation-grade, engraved to a battle that history doesn't record
A strongbox of assay reports, every one for the same claim, every one different
$40 in scrip on a bank three days burned
A bridal trunk, packed with care, smelling of cedar; the dress has been buried once
A preacher's traveling kit — Bible, salt, iron nails — worn to the shape of hard use
A spyglass that shows the country as it was: greener, watered, peopled
Letters of credit signed by a man the papers hanged last spring
A twist of tobacco, a good knife, and a map inked on a scraped hide, older than any survey
A marshal's badge, tarnished black, pinned to nothing but a scrap of shirt
A music box that plays a tune nobody in the party has ever heard, and hums under their breath after
$15 in silver dollars, each one stamped with a year that hasn't happened yet
A set of dentures, gold-capped, wrapped in a handkerchief with someone else's initials
A folded map with a town on it that isn't on any other map
A rifle scope that shows the target already dead, whether it is or not
A rancher's tally book, every brand crossed out but one
A rosary, each bead a different faith's, strung on the same cord
A land grant, stamped and legal, for ground currently underwater
A pocket watch that runs backward exactly one hour a day, no more
omens: The team balks at the ford; the water runs clear over nothing
A ring around the moon, doubled
The windmill turns hard against the wind, groaning
Every portrait in the parlor has been turned to the wall
The church door stands open at midnight, lamplit, empty
A coyote sits the ridge at noon, watching, and the dogs won't sound
Fresh-baked bread on the sill of a house dark for years
The party's names in the hotel register, in yesterday's ink
A scarecrow gone from its cross, and footprints leading away
The weathervane points at the party all day, whatever the wind
A crow lands on the ridgepole and won't be shot, shooed, or ignored
The well rope comes up wet with something that isn't water
A newborn calf born with its eyes already open, watching
The family Bible falls open to the same page no matter how it's shelved
A stray dog howls at noon, once, and then never barks again
Every mirror in the house needs re-silvering within the week
The preacher's shadow lags a half-step behind him at the pulpit
A rocking horse rocks itself to a stop only when watched
The chickens roost at dawn instead of dusk, refusing to be moved
A wedding ring turns up in the flour bin, engraved with a stranger's names
The porch steps creak in order, heel to toe, when nobody's on them
A photograph in the parlor develops a new face in the back row each week
